name: Architecture Foundations (Dennemeyer)
description: Microservices boundaries, DDD/CQRS, SOLID and layered/clean architecture, containers and the Kubernetes runtime, and the architect's working practice (design docs, NFRs, testing strategy, safe DB migration, technical leadership).

topics.name: Microservices, DDD & Service Boundaries
topics.description: When services earn their independence: ownership, bounded contexts, CQRS, and Conway's Law.
topics.studyNotes:
A microservice is a separately deployable service with its own responsibility. It is justified when there is clean ownership, an API contract, a data boundary, monitoring, and a deployment pipeline behind it. The goal is NOT that everything be small — too many services increase latency and make debugging painful. Payment, document generation, and notification make sense as separate services IF they scale separately and separate teams own them.

DDD supplies the boundary language: organize code around business concepts — bounded contexts, entities, value objects, aggregates, domain services. In an IP domain the concepts are trademark, patent, renewal, portfolio, payment — and a Renewal is not a DB row but a business concept with rules: when it can start, who is entitled, what states it has. Understand the domain language first, then shape modules around it.

Conway's Law is the organizational check: system structure mirrors team communication. Badly drawn team boundaries produce badly drawn service boundaries — so service design is an ownership and communication question, not only a technical one. Separate payment and IP-workflow teams → a clean API boundary between them usually beats one shared giant service.

CQRS separates the write model from the read model: commands mutate under complex business rules; queries read from a fast, denormalized read model (customer dashboard). It is NOT a default — only where read and write needs genuinely diverge enough to justify two models and the eventual consistency between them.

Say-out-loud line from the source: "I would use CQRS only where read and write needs are really different enough to justify it."

topics.name: SOLID, Layered & Clean Architecture
topics.description: Design principles and patterns as means, not ends; layer responsibilities; framework-independent domains.
topics.studyNotes:
SOLID is not five definitions to memorize — it is one goal: small-responsibility, loosely coupled, easily testable modules. The example that lands: a RenewalService should not also generate PDFs, send emails, and process payments — those are separate responsibilities (Single Responsibility). At architect level the same principle applies to SERVICES: clean responsibility, few hidden dependencies.

OOA/OOD is the order of operations: analysis first — understand the domain concepts and their relations (Customer, Portfolio, Trademark) — then design objects, interfaces, modules and responsibilities from them. Business language before code.

Design patterns are proven solutions to recurring problems — and never a goal in themselves. Use them when they SIMPLIFY: Strategy for a swappable algorithm, Adapter to fit external systems (one partner sends XML, another JSON — adapters map both to one internal model), Repository to hide data access. The interview line: "I use patterns when they simplify the code, not for the sake of patterns."

Layered architecture assigns responsibilities: controller/API (request handling only — no business logic in controllers), application/service, domain, infrastructure/data. Clean architecture adds the dependency rule: the domain depends on NO framework, database, or UI — infrastructure connects through adapters/interfaces (the domain never imports Entity Framework or PostgreSQL details; data access hides behind a repository interface). Worth its ceremony when the domain is complex and long-lived; overkill for a thin CRUD service.

topics.name: Containers & the Kubernetes Runtime
topics.description: Docker/K8s deployment safety, service discovery, gateway vs load balancer, ingress, reverse proxies.
topics.studyNotes:
Docker packages the app into an image; Kubernetes runs, scales, restarts and rolls out the containers. The working vocabulary: deployment, service, ingress, configmap, secret, readiness/liveness probes, rolling update, rollback. The theme an interviewer wants to hear is DEPLOYMENT SAFETY: during a rollout Kubernetes starts new pods, sends traffic only after readiness passes, then gradually drains the old ones — combined with graceful shutdown, that is zero-downtime.

How traffic finds the service:
- Service discovery: pods have changing IPs; you call http://payment-service by NAME and Kubernetes routes to current pods.
- Load balancer: distributes traffic across instances/pods, drops unhealthy ones (readiness false → no traffic), enables rolling updates. Stateless services make this trivial.
- API Gateway: an API CONTROL POINT — auth, rate limiting, routing, transformation, versioning, monitoring. The distinction to make: external customer APIs sit behind a gateway; internally a load balancer just spreads pods. Gateway = policy, LB = distribution.
- Reverse proxy (NGINX, Ingress): the intermediate layer doing TLS termination, compression, request-size limits, auth pre-filtering, routing.
- Kubernetes Ingress: the cluster's external HTTP routing rules — api.company.com/renewals → renewal-api-service; Service is internal pod access, Ingress is external entry.

.NET-awareness footnote from the source (transferable to any stack): know the platform's web framework, ORM, middleware pipeline and DI story well enough to discuss architecture, and say honestly that implementation details would be ramp-up.

topics.name: The Architect's Working Practice
topics.description: Design documents, NFRs first, testing strategy, safe schema migration, technical leadership.
topics.studyNotes:
Non-functional requirements come BEFORE architecture: latency, availability, scalability, security, auditability, maintainability, cost. "A renewals API" is not a requirement — daily request volume, latency expectation, audit needs, and tolerable downtime are. The interview line: "Before choosing architecture I would clarify traffic, latency, security, audit and availability requirements." Availability is a business decision on that list: 99.9% is ~43 minutes of downtime a month; 99.99% is dramatically stricter and more expensive — an internal admin report does not need what the customer portal needs.

The system design document is a short decision-support artifact: goal, scope, requirements, NFRs, diagram, API contract, data model, risks, ALTERNATIVES, rollout plan. Its purpose is to make the design understandable and debatable — e.g. why queue-based async document generation over a sync call, with rollback and monitoring steps.

Testing strategy by layer: unit tests for small logic, integration tests for DB/API edges, contract tests for API contracts (they catch the backend renaming status to state before customers do), E2E for whole flows. TDD — test first — is a tool for clarifying expected behaviour, not a dogma.

Safe database migration is a choreography, not an ALTER: add the new (nullable) field → app writes both forms → backfill → app reads the new field → retire the old one later. Production never breaks because each step is backward compatible.

Technical leadership is not people management: clarify requirements, compare design options, name risks out loud, slice work into small deliverables (API contract, mapping, validation, async worker, monitoring, rollout), review code, unblock the team, and communicate with product and infra.

questions.title: Drawing service boundaries
questions.prompt: The team wants to split a monolith into microservices. How do you decide where the boundaries go — and when would you say no?
questions.answerStructureHint: Boundary criteria (ownership, data, contract) → domain language / bounded contexts → Conway's Law → costs of too many services.

expectedPoints.label: Boundary criteria
expectedPoints.description: Clean ownership, API contract, data boundary, separate scaling, monitoring + deploy pipeline.

expectedPoints.label: Domain-driven boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Bounded contexts around business concepts; domain language first.

expectedPoints.label: Conway's Law
expectedPoints.description: Team communication shapes the system; boundaries are an org question too.

expectedPoints.label: Names the costs
expectedPoints.description: Too many services → latency, debugging pain; monolith/modular monolith can be right.

followUps.prompt: Where does CQRS fit into this picture, and when would you avoid it?

expectedPoints.label: CQRS judgment
expectedPoints.description: Separate write/read models only when needs genuinely diverge; brings eventual consistency.

questions.title: Where does the business logic live?
questions.prompt: Describe how you layer a backend service and why the domain should not depend on the framework or database.

expectedPoints.label: Layer responsibilities
expectedPoints.description: Controller handles requests only; service/application orchestrates; domain holds rules; infrastructure adapts.
acceptedSignals: no business logic in the controller

expectedPoints.label: Dependency rule
expectedPoints.description: Domain framework/DB/UI-independent; adapters + repository interfaces at the edges.

expectedPoints.label: Principles as means
expectedPoints.description: SOLID = small responsibility + loose coupling + testability; patterns only where they simplify.

questions.title: Gateway, load balancer, ingress — who does what?
questions.prompt: In a Kubernetes deployment, explain the difference between an API gateway, a load balancer, an ingress, and service discovery — and what makes a rollout zero-downtime.

expectedPoints.label: Gateway = policy, LB = distribution
expectedPoints.description: Gateway: auth/rate limit/routing/versioning for external APIs; LB spreads pods and drops unhealthy ones.

expectedPoints.label: Discovery + ingress
expectedPoints.description: Call services by name (payment-service); Ingress routes external HTTP into the cluster.

expectedPoints.label: Zero-downtime mechanics
expectedPoints.description: Readiness gates traffic to new pods; graceful shutdown drains old ones; rollback available.

questions.title: From feature request to design
questions.prompt: Product asks for a new integration feature. Walk me through what you do before any code is written, as the technical lead.
questions.answerStructureHint: NFRs first → short design doc with alternatives → testing strategy → slicing into deliverables + rollout.

expectedPoints.label: NFRs before architecture
expectedPoints.description: Traffic, latency, security, audit, availability clarified first; availability is a business call.

expectedPoints.label: Design doc with alternatives
expectedPoints.description: Goal, scope, contract, data model, risks, alternatives, rollout — understandable and debatable.

expectedPoints.label: Slicing + leadership
expectedPoints.description: Small deliverables (contract, mapping, validation, worker, monitoring); unblock, review, communicate.

expectedPoints.label: Safe DB migration pattern
expectedPoints.description: Add nullable → write both → backfill → read new → retire old.
